ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai boat noodle with crispy chicken. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai boat noodle. A00LC (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible parent category for this noodle soup dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Thai noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of crispy chicken boat noodle as served in a Thai restaurant, approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, crispy chicken pieces, broth, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Boat noodle soup with chicken (kuai tiao ruea) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for rice noodle soup with chicken and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian noodle soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for crispy chicken boat noodle exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for rice noodles (cooked), chicken (fried/breaded), and a dark-soy-fish-sauce broth profile consistent with Thai boat noodle preparations documented in FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. The crispy coating adds fat and energy relative to plain boiled chicken. Per-100 g values are derived from a modelled 450 g bowl: approximately 180 g cooked rice noodles, 120 g crispy fried chicken, 130 g broth with vegetables, scaled to 100 g.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to boat noodle dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 53–61 for boiled rice noodles (mean ~55, low category). Crispy chicken boat noodle is dominated by rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate source. The fat and protein from the crispy chicken and broth are expected to modestly attenuate glycaemic response. A GI of 55 (low) is applied as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: (55 × 57.6 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 31.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, dark soy sauce, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 2160 mg per 450 g bowl), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index rice noodles with moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as the rice noodle base carries a low GI of approximately 55 and delivers a glycemic load of approximately 31.7 per bowl, which may cause a significant postprandial glucose rise; portion control and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and saturated fat from deep-fried chicken coating and seasoned broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently due to its high sodium content and the saturated fat contributed by the deep-fried crispy chicken, both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Chicken Boat Noodle (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือไก่กรอบ, Kuai Tiao Ruea Kai Krob) is a Thai noodle soup dish that combines the bold, deeply savoury flavours of traditional Thai boat noodle broth with golden deep-fried crispy chicken. Boat noodle soup (kuai tiao ruea) is a classic Thai street food originating from the floating markets of central Thailand, traditionally made with a rich, dark broth seasoned with fish sauce, dark soy sauce, dried spices, and sometimes pork blood, giving it an intensely umami, slightly sweet, and aromatic character. In this variant, crispy fried chicken replaces or supplements the traditional pork or beef, offering a contrasting crunchy texture against the soft, slippery rice noodles and the liquid broth. The dish is typically served with bean sprouts, morning glory (water spinach), fried garlic, dried chilli flakes, and a condiment tray of fish sauce, sugar, vinegar, and chilli for individual seasoning. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from the chicken (approximately 8.2 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from rice noodles (approximately 12.8 g per 100 g), and elevated sodium from the seasoned broth (approximately 480 mg per 100 g). The deep-fried chicken coating contributes additional fat and energy. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on the rice noodle base. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and the moderate glycemic load warrants portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or fish-derived condiments.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for crispy chicken boat noodle exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by modelling the dish from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked rice noodles and fried chicken, combined with a Thai boat noodle broth profile consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA documentation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in fried chicken and rice noodle preparations per USDA SR Legacy data. The dish is served at Saap Saap Thai - Funan (Singapore) as menu item N12. Halal status is not confirmed for this restaurant; is_halal is set to false pending verification. The per-100 g values are modelled from a 450 g bowl composition estimate and carry inherent uncertainty; a direct laboratory analysis or approved source entry would improve confidence to high.
